item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Necroposting this one somewhat… but I only check back on this thing extremely infrequently so this is the first time I'm seeing this comment >.>

The intended meaning of "similar circumstances" was actually really boring. It was more along the lines of the fact that every set of memories involves having just touched the coin, and in particular, if the coin had stayed in one geographic place for a long period of time, the memories would also share that in common. But I absolutely love your interpretation of it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi there! Man, it HAS been a while since I was around, hasn't it? I do drop back in on this to see the comments, uh, very occasionally, and it's pretty heartwarming to see there's still a little bit of interest around it!

I agree completely that this SCP would benefit from testing logs and/or perhaps an exploration log that goes into how the Foundation discovered and acquired it. But I never felt confident enough to write any myself. I did lay out some lines of thought in an earlier comment, which could be possible starting points, if anyone wanted to take them and run with them. http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-462368/scp-1072#post-1421114

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given that the author has not posted since 2012, It will probably be up to others to fix this one up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mirroring a lot of the feedback here, I'd say that this article lacks a finishing polish. As it stands, it's too simple to enjoy. I'd love it if there was a twist with the device and the stored memories. Maybe a copy is stored into the disk for everyone to be eternally trapped. An exploration log or tests would make it upvote worthy.

I hope in the future, someone come back and tries their hand at modifying the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not terrible but definately needs some work, an interview with test subjects, maybe physical testing of the object (what's it made out of? Can it be etched into? Does it serve some purpose or could it be useful in some way?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was kind of hoping that this thing was swapping minds across parallel worlds - there was something evocative about the detail of multiple activations producing minds with similar circumstances, like they're all coming from only slightly divergent worlds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My main problem is that we already have a lot of things that alter memories, I think it is a little cliche… I also tought that the expungement was not the best…
No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was expecting for something that never came. It feels a little incomplete. Maybe a test log could help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I see what's going on here, but the problem is the very abstract and internal effects require some really convoluted explanation, so… it's convoluted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kind of boring… Seems half-finished to boot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't this just Whitesnake from Jojo's Bizarre Adventure part 6 with a compulsion effect?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair questions. I don't really have concrete answers, but I have come up with some lines of thought on the matter.

Firstly, we don't know whether walls interrupt the ten metre radius. Keeping it in a safe may not protect people walking along nearby corridors. And the Foundation are probably still conducting some experiments on it, even if not with materials, so they'd probably prefer to just keep it in one room that's secure rather than moving an object with a ten metre radius of effect around the facility every time they want to poke at it. Secondly, as to the question of line of sight — the implication is that sight is the operative motivator but again, nothing I wrote explicitly demands it. Naturally, there's a potential for test logs with both of these!

Really, what I think it comes down to is that the containment is perhaps larger than strictly necessary, but it's hardly excessive and convoluted. A big room, a few metres of wire, a few servo motors and other little electronic oddments. That's pretty much it. I doubt that put any visible dent at all in the Foundation's budget.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The containment procedure seems odd to me. rather than 20m room with remote manipulators why is it not just kept in a locked safe in small locked room? If people can't see it do they still have compulsion? The compulsion is said to be easy to resist anyway so doesn't sound like people will be beating their head on the door trying to get in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://pastebin.com/jhMWwwrA If you're interested… But yeah, it's inferior. It was a lot more heavy-handed about redacted stuff, and the effects were more convoluted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't read the original, but this is excellent. And that idea just makes it even more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On first touching? Maybe just wiped. But presumably they would have gone on to touch it more than once, and thus gotten their own memory back, as it was the only one stored. Unless of course this thing was built with some memories in it already. Possibly even those of the creator. For all we know, a future test subject might one day inherit the knowledge of how this thing came to be. Depends how many memories are stored on it. And whether they are stored indefinitely.

What you also have to wonder is, how did the Foundation find it? And would it have been surrounded by brain-damaged and [DATA EXPUNGED] corpses? There may be a log to be written there, but I don't think I'm the one to write it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I gave this a quick glance as originally written but not enough to really get into it. Read through now, and the extra hook of it randomly swapping your mind with someone else whose head was emptied out previously was enough to grab me.

Of course…. Now I'm curious as to what happened to the *first* person who touched it..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great clinical tone and highly appropriate containment procedures and testing. The horror suffers a little from it, but overall this is a quality scp article. Upvoted.

item.title: Re: New image
item.content:encoded: Looks good to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This rewrite is much better. Upvoted,

item.title: Re: New image
item.content:encoded: I kind of like that one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually find this… not terrifying or really all that scary, but creepy and intriguing. And to me, not EVERY SCP should be scary. Sure the scary ones are fun, but so is stuff like this.

item.title: New image
item.content:encoded:
Okay, since the previous image wasn't great, and also since I've changed the description of the object a little, I've done a new image for potential use on the page. Thoughts on this one?

http://i759.photobucket.com/albums/xx238/SorcerorNobody/SCP-1072.png

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Digging the rewrite. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Trimmed a few bits that added relatively little, and broke up some long sentences. Let me know if there are any particular segments you think still need further clinical treatment.

And for the record, boy :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True, albeit my original belief was that this could be quite fun … Intriguing works too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Plenty of SCPs survive on intrigue rather than horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is better written than before. It's now simpler, but I still feel the focus isn't the horror. I may be subjective though. Tentative upvote. I will note that you do love spamming things that need commas and commas in general, marking the article seem a lot less clinical and a lot more narrative. Get your word scythe out and start cutting, boy(Or girl)!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As suggested, starting afresh with the rewritten version of what was previously SCP-740. I daresay some issues remain with it, of course, but hopefully it might get off to a slightly better start this time.
